questions.question: In which case did Lord Langdale classically articulate the three certainties required for a valid express trust?

options.A: McPhail v Doulton [1971]
options.B: Knight v Knight [1840]
options.C: IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]
options.D: Re Gulbenkian's Settlements [1970]

questions.explanation: The three certainties were classically articulated by Lord Langdale in Knight v Knight [1840]. He held that for a valid trust to exist, there must be certainty of intention, certainty of subject matter, and certainty of objects. If any one of these three is missing, the trust will fail.

questions.question: Words such as 'I wish', 'I hope', and 'in full confidence that' are classified in trust law as:

options.A: Imperative words that create a binding trust obligation
options.B: Precatory words that are merely expressions of hope and do not create a trust
options.C: Conditional words that create a trust only if the condition is satisfied
options.D: Hybrid words that require extrinsic evidence to determine their legal effect

questions.explanation: Words such as 'I wish', 'I hope', and 'in full confidence that' are classified as precatory words. They are merely expressions of hope and do not, without more, create a trust obligation. By contrast, imperative words such as 'I direct', 'I require', or 'on trust for' clearly create a binding trust obligation.

questions.question: In Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry [1884], a testator left property to his wife 'in full confidence that she will do what is right.' What was the court's decision?

options.A: The words created a valid express trust because 'full confidence' is imperative
options.B: The words were precatory only and did not create a trust — the wife took absolutely
options.C: The trust was valid but subject to the cy-pres doctrine
options.D: The trust was void for uncertainty of objects because 'do what is right' was too vague

questions.explanation: In Re Adams and the Kensington Vestry [1884], the Court of Appeal held that the words 'in full confidence that she will do what is right' were precatory only and did not create a trust. The wife took the property absolutely. This is the leading case on the distinction between imperative and precatory words in certainty of intention.

questions.question: In Comiskey v Bowring-Hanbury [1905], the House of Lords held that precatory language could create a trust when:

options.A: The testator used the word 'trust' at least once in the will
options.B: The will, read as a whole, showed a clear intention to impose a binding obligation
options.C: The precatory words were accompanied by a signed attestation
options.D: The beneficiaries were named with sufficient certainty to identify them

questions.explanation: In Comiskey v Bowring-Hanbury [1905], the House of Lords held that even precatory language could create a trust when the will, read as a whole, showed a clear intention to impose a binding obligation. Context matters — the court looks at the totality of the language used, not just isolated words, to determine whether a trust obligation was intended.

questions.question: In Palmer v Simmonds [1854], a trust over 'the bulk of my residuary estate' failed because:

options.A: The beneficiaries were not named with sufficient precision
options.B: The subject matter was too vague — 'the bulk' could not be ascertained with precision
options.C: The trust was not evidenced in writing as required by the Statute of Frauds
options.D: The trust offended the rule against perpetuities

questions.explanation: In Palmer v Simmonds [1854], a trust over 'the bulk of my residuary estate' failed for uncertainty of subject matter. The word 'bulk' was too vague — it was impossible to determine with precision what proportion of the estate it referred to. This is the leading case on certainty of subject matter.

questions.question: In Re Golay's Will Trusts [1965], a direction to allow a beneficiary to enjoy 'a reasonable income' was upheld because:

options.A: The court imputed an exact figure using actuarial evidence
options.B: 'Reasonable' was an objective standard the court itself could determine
options.C: The beneficiary and trustee agreed on the amount without court intervention
options.D: The direction fell within the cy-pres doctrine as a charitable gift

questions.explanation: In Re Golay's Will Trusts [1965], the court held that 'a reasonable income' was an objective standard that the court itself could determine, and therefore the subject matter was sufficiently certain. This contrasts with 'the bulk' in Palmer v Simmonds, which had no objective standard by which it could be measured.

questions.question: In Re London Wine Company [1986], the trust failed for want of certainty of subject matter because:

options.A: The wine had been consumed before the trust was established
options.B: The specific bottles allocated to each buyer could not be identified as they had not been segregated from the general pool
options.C: The company lacked legal capacity to create a trust over its stock in trade
options.D: The beneficiaries were not identified with sufficient certainty

questions.explanation: In Re London Wine Company [1986], Oliver J held the trust failed for want of certainty of subject matter because the specific bottles of wine allocated to each buyer could not be identified — they had not been segregated from the general pool. Without identification and segregation of the specific trust property, no valid trust arose.

questions.question: How did Hunter v Moss [1994] distinguish Re London Wine Company [1986]?

options.A: Hunter v Moss involved land rather than personal property
options.B: In Hunter v Moss, the shares were identical and fungible, removing the need to identify specific shares
options.C: In Hunter v Moss, the trust property had been physically segregated before the trust was declared
options.D: Hunter v Moss involved a discretionary trust whereas Re London Wine involved a fixed trust

questions.explanation: In Hunter v Moss [1994], the English Court of Appeal held that a trust over 50 shares out of 950 identical shares was valid even without segregation of the specific shares, because the shares were identical and fungible. Since any 50 shares would be equivalent to any other 50 shares, there was no need to identify specific shares. This distinguishes it from Re London Wine where individual bottles differed.

questions.question: For a fixed trust, the test for certainty of objects is:

options.A: The is or is not test from McPhail v Doulton [1971]
options.B: The complete list test from IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]
options.C: The substantial number test proposed by Megaw LJ in Re Baden No. 2 [1973]
options.D: The reasonable ascertainability test from Re Gulbenkian's Settlements [1970]

questions.explanation: For a fixed trust — where trustees must distribute to specific named beneficiaries in fixed shares — the test for certainty of objects is the complete list test from IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]. The trustee must be able to draw up a complete list of all beneficiaries. If a complete list cannot be drawn up, the trust fails.

questions.question: In McPhail v Doulton [1971], Lord Wilberforce replaced which test for discretionary trusts?

options.A: The is or is not test, replacing it with the complete list test
options.B: The complete list test, replacing it with the is or is not test
options.C: The substantial number test, replacing it with the reasonable ascertainability test
options.D: The conceptual certainty test, replacing it with the evidential certainty test

questions.explanation: In McPhail v Doulton (Re Baden No. 1) [1971], the House of Lords through Lord Wilberforce replaced the complete list test with the 'is or is not' test for discretionary trusts. Under this test, a discretionary trust is valid if it can be said with certainty whether any given individual is or is not a member of the class. A complete list is not required.

questions.question: In Re Baden No. 2 [1973], Sachs LJ's approach to the 'is or is not' test was that:

options.A: The trust is valid only if a substantial number of people clearly fall within the class
options.B: If a person cannot prove they are within the class, they are to be treated as outside it
options.C: The strict is or is not test must be applied to every individual
options.D: The class must be capable of being listed in full within a reasonable time

questions.explanation: In Re Baden No. 2 [1973], Sachs LJ held that if a person could not prove they were within the class, they were to be treated as outside it. This is the most permissive approach of the three judges. Megaw LJ held the trust valid if a substantial number clearly fell within the class; Stamp LJ applied the strict is or is not test.

questions.question: The test for certainty of objects for a power of appointment is:

options.A: The complete list test from IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust
options.B: The is or is not test from Re Gulbenkian's Settlements [1970]
options.C: A lower threshold — any conceptually identifiable class will suffice
options.D: No certainty of objects is required for a power of appointment

questions.explanation: The test for certainty of objects for a power of appointment is the same as for a discretionary trust — the is or is not test, established in Re Gulbenkian's Settlements [1970]. This makes sense because both discretionary trusts and powers of appointment involve a selection from a class.

questions.question: Which of the following class descriptions would MOST LIKELY fail the certainty of objects test for a discretionary trust?

options.A: The children of John Smith
options.B: Employees of XYZ Ltd
options.C: My friends
options.D: Residents of Dublin at the date of my death

questions.explanation: 'My friends' would most likely fail the certainty of objects test because it is conceptually uncertain — there is no objective criterion by which 'friend' can be defined. A person cannot be identified with any certainty as a friend or non-friend. By contrast, 'children of John Smith', 'employees of XYZ Ltd', and 'residents of Dublin' are all conceptually certain, even if evidential difficulties may arise.

questions.question: In the context of the three certainties, what is the distinction between conceptual certainty and evidential certainty?

options.A: Conceptual certainty relates to the written terms and evidential certainty relates to oral terms
options.B: Conceptual certainty means the class is linguistically clear; evidential certainty concerns whether a given person can be proven to fall within the class
options.C: Conceptual certainty is required only for fixed trusts; evidential certainty is required for discretionary trusts
options.D: Both terms mean the same thing and are used interchangeably in the case law

questions.explanation: Conceptual certainty means the definition of the class is linguistically clear — there is an objective criterion by which membership can be assessed. Evidential certainty concerns whether, in practice, it can be proven whether a given person falls within the class. For the is or is not test, conceptual certainty is required; evidential difficulties alone do not invalidate the trust.

questions.question: The Irish case of Re Hamilton [1895] confirmed which principle regarding certainty of intention?

options.A: That the word 'trust' must appear in the instrument for certainty of intention to be established
options.B: That each case must be decided on the particular language used and the circumstances as a whole
options.C: That precatory words in an Irish will always create a valid trust obligation
options.D: That certainty of intention requires corroboration from an independent witness

questions.explanation: Re Hamilton [1895] confirmed the Irish position that each case on certainty of intention must be decided on the particular language used and the circumstances as a whole. The court emphasised that there is no single formula — the key question is always whether the person intended to create a legally binding obligation or merely expressed a wish.

questions.question: If a testator leaves '£10,000 to be divided among those of my employees who have been loyal to me', what is the MOST likely certainty of objects problem?

options.A: The subject matter is uncertain because £10,000 cannot be divided precisely
options.B: The class 'loyal employees' is conceptually uncertain — 'loyal' has no objective standard
options.C: Employees as a class is too large to constitute valid objects
options.D: A testamentary trust cannot be created over money as a matter of law

questions.explanation: The most likely certainty of objects problem is that the class 'loyal employees' is conceptually uncertain — 'loyal' is a subjective quality with no objective standard by which the class can be defined. It is impossible to say with certainty whether any given employee is or is not a 'loyal' employee, which fails the is or is not test for discretionary trusts.

questions.question: Both aspects of certainty of subject matter relate to which two things?

options.A: The legal title to the property and the equitable title to the property
options.B: The trust property itself and the beneficial interests of the beneficiaries
options.C: The nature of the property and the location of the property
options.D: The present value of the property and its future value

questions.explanation: Certainty of subject matter has two aspects: (1) the trust property itself must be clearly identified, and (2) the beneficial interests of the beneficiaries must also be certain. Both must be satisfied. A trust may fail if the trust property is sufficiently identified but the shares or interests of the beneficiaries are uncertain.

questions.question: Which of the following CORRECTLY describes the significance of McPhail v Doulton [1971] in the development of trust law?

options.A: It abolished the distinction between fixed and discretionary trusts for certainty purposes
options.B: It replaced the complete list test with the is or is not test for discretionary trusts, expanding the range of valid trusts
options.C: It held that discretionary trusts require a complete list of beneficiaries like fixed trusts
options.D: It applied the is or is not test to fixed trusts as well as discretionary trusts

questions.explanation: McPhail v Doulton [1971] is significant because it replaced the more restrictive complete list test with the is or is not test for discretionary trusts. This expanded the range of valid discretionary trusts — many trusts that would previously have failed for inability to compile a complete list could now be upheld, provided conceptual certainty existed.

questions.question: A settlor declares a trust over 'some of my shares in ABC plc' without specifying the number. Which certainty is most likely deficient?

options.A: Certainty of intention
options.B: Certainty of subject matter
options.C: Certainty of objects
options.D: No certainty is deficient — 'some shares' is sufficiently precise

questions.explanation: Certainty of subject matter is deficient. 'Some of my shares' is too vague — the number of shares that forms the trust property cannot be ascertained. This is analogous to the 'bulk' problem in Palmer v Simmonds [1854]. Without knowing how many shares are subject to the trust, the trust property is not clearly identified and the trust fails.

questions.question: Which of the three certainties is most directly addressed by the distinction between 'I direct my trustees' and 'I hope my trustees will'?

options.A: Certainty of subject matter
options.B: Certainty of objects
options.C: Certainty of intention
options.D: All three certainties equally

questions.explanation: The distinction between 'I direct my trustees' (imperative — creates a binding obligation) and 'I hope my trustees will' (precatory — merely a wish) directly addresses certainty of intention. The question is whether the settlor intended to create a legally binding trust obligation or merely expressed a hope or desire.

questions.question: If any one of the three certainties is absent from an express trust, what is the legal consequence?

options.A: The trust is voidable at the option of the trustees
options.B: The trust fails and the property results back to the settlor or their estate
options.C: The court may supply the missing certainty using its inherent jurisdiction
options.D: The trust remains valid but the trustees are personally liable for any breach

questions.explanation: If any one of the three certainties is absent, the trust fails. The legal consequence is that the property results back to the settlor or, if the trust was testamentary, to the testator's estate on a resulting trust. The court cannot supply the missing certainty — it is for the settlor to satisfy all three requirements.

questions.question: An FE-1 problem question describes a trust for 'my relatives who have been kind to me'. Applying the three certainties, which analysis is CORRECT?

options.A: The trust is valid because 'relatives' is a sufficiently certain class
options.B: The trust fails for uncertainty of subject matter as 'kind' has no fixed monetary value
options.C: The trust likely fails for uncertainty of objects — 'kind to me' is conceptually uncertain even though 'relatives' is not
options.D: The trust is valid as a discretionary trust because the complete list test does not apply

questions.explanation: The trust likely fails for uncertainty of objects. While 'relatives' may be a sufficiently certain class, 'who have been kind to me' introduces a conceptually uncertain qualifier — there is no objective standard by which kindness can be assessed. It cannot be said with certainty whether any given relative is or is not within this sub-class, failing the is or is not test for discretionary trusts.
